speaker: interviewer
text: Please continue with your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I feel a little better now that we had a break, because I was trying to get-- to hold back. But it's-- reliving it again is very, very difficult, and I just find it quite hard to continue. But I'll try. I'll try and continue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And have some water if you'd like and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: We're very patient here.

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband told me not to break, and I'm trying not to break while I'm giving this interview.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, he's not here. So you should just tell the story, and whatever comes out, comes out. And he's not here, so he'll never know.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- the SS men came into the room, to Srodula, Ghetto Srodula, and they took me away out of my mother's bed. It was a terrible experience for me. I didn't know whether I should cry, but my mother told me, [NON-ENGLISH], go. Go. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I listened to what my mother told me. They loaded me onto a bus where there were other girls. And on the way, we-- we talked, and we were scared. We didn't know where we were going, where they were taking us. But there were rumors going around that they're taking us to a place where they took the girls who were making uniforms for the Germans. And at that stage, I-- I thought that-- I don't know. I hoped they would take us to where my sisters were taken. As we arrived into a certain camp, we were told to get off the bus. And there were some girls in-- in the camp who worked night shift and stayed home during the day, and they asked me what my name was. And when I told them Sarah Zielinska, they embraced me and they hugged me and they told me that my sisters were there. I was overcome with emotion, and I thanked God that I would be together with my older sisters. My sisters were work-- at work at the time--

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- which sisters were these now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Esther and Manja. And I couldn't wait to see them. And about 6 or 8 o'clock in the evening, I heard them coming. And I stood outside in the yard of the camp. And we were extremely happy. My sisters were happy to see me, and I was very, very happy to be with my older sisters. And when we were given the soup, a bowl of soup, I saw some meat there, which I didn't think I should eat because it wasn't kosher. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they told me, Sally, try and eat. And when I saw them eat the unkosher food, I was surprised. I was very surprised that they were doing that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But they-- they continued doing it, and I just couldn't understand it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just ask you a couple questions, and then we'll continue. How old were you at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was not quite 13.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when they took you out of your mother's bed in-- in night, did they give you a chance to change clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just put on the clothes that I wore the-- the previous day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long a bus ride was it from Sosnowiec to--

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't a full day, I-- because I think we arrived-- I'm not quite sure. I think we arrived the same day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said, the camp. And what was the name of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: The camp was Graeben, and it was near Breslau, and it was also near Gross-Rosen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your sisters have now told you to break the laws of kosher. And when did you-- did you-- did you do it at that time, or did you wait, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I couldn't do it for a couple of weeks until I-- I felt that I was weakening. And I-- at first, I would give my meat to my sisters, throw it in my sisters' bowls. And they weren't happy that I was doing it. But after a while, I felt that-- I was assigned to work, which the younger girls were assigned to. There were about four or five girls of my age in that camp. Most of the girls were older. They were in their late teens or-- or early 20s. And when I started weakening, I felt that I would have the meat. At first, it was disgusting. It was just-- I felt that I was going to throw it up. But after a while I got used to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the barracks in which you lived. Did you live with your older sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in the same room. There were about 15 girls assigned. There were bunks, and I had the lower bunk, and my older sisters had the upper bunk. We had a bunk on our own. I had a bunk of my own. And we had little cupboards also, and little wardrobes, part wardrobes, where we could hang our clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me more about-- tell me more about the sanitation.

speaker: interviewee
text: The sanitation wasn't bad. We showered every day-- we-- after work, because our work was very dirty work. It was a flax factory. Flax is a-- a kind of a straw-looking material from which they were making yarn. In our place we were making yarn. And from there they sent it to textile mills to make fabrics from.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many girls worked in this factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were about 250 girls. There were some girls working-- every day we were called in the morning to report in the backyard. It was called [GERMAN]. And we were-- names were called out, and we had to acknowledge our presence. And then we were marched to work by a German guard to the flax factory. And at first I was assigned to a part of the factory, a lighter-- lighter work. And my two sisters worked in a part of the factory was very wet flax, which was heavier to carry, the-- as my department was dry flax, which wasn't so heavy. And we would load it on conveyor belt to different parts of the factory. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after a while, I decided that I wanted to be with my sisters. And they assigned me to their work. But I preferred to work harder and be with my sisters rather than being assigned to a lighter job, which I carried out pretty adequately. But later on, it was a little harder. It was much harder. But the fact that I could be with my sisters meant a lot to me and to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned you were marched and there was a guard. Were you-- were you guarded during the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were kind of-- there was a-- a meister, a German meister, who was standing and watching us work. All the time we had to perform. And they-- there were also different people at that factory. There were German women working the easier job that I had. German women would come to that place. It was, I think, a private corporation, privately owned flax factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know who owned it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were given a name, because my sister suffered from-- her hands would rot from the wet flax, and the-- the person who owned the factory came to look at her hands at one time. And I don't recall the name of the owner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your sister receive medical treatment?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall that. I'm not quite sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the workers mistreated at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, we were treated the worst, Jewish people. And Russian prisoners of war were also at that factory. But there were some Polish workers who were allowed to go home for Christmas. And there were also Czechoslovakian men, grown men, who would walk by our part of work, the work which we were doing. And one Czechoslovakian man looked at my sister. She was a very attractive teenager, and he just looked up like that at her. And the following day, he slipped a little note where my sister was on, and he wrote-- he made a kind of a-- on that slip was a picture of a package, and a place where he would leave that package where my sister Mary was working. And the following day-- and he said to go there and to pick up a package, which my sister Mary did, and it was bread. We were very happy to receive. And it was of a different-- made of different flour than the camp bread. It was a lighter color. And when we would go back home to the barracks, we were delighted to have such a tasty bread, because the bread which we had in camp wasn't tasty. But it was tasty enough to eat when we were hungry. But this bread was much tastier.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did this man ever help you or your sisters again?

speaker: interviewee
text: This man, in fact, continued helping us on-- for a while. And we, in the exchange, wanted to do something for this man. We had a knitted dress made of-- crocheted dress of my sister's, and I remember a gray dress-- and for the holidays I and my sister helped to knit a pullover for him, and we gave it to him. It was also very discreetly because we were told not to mix with any other people but our own. And this continued for several months, and that helped us a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get the materials to knit the pullover with?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the dress.

speaker: interviewer
text: From--

speaker: interviewee
text: We undid the dress at night. We didn't sleep at night, and we undid the dress by hand, and then we started-- and when you undo handmade fabric, you can make another fabric. You can make another garment out of the same yarn.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the knitting needles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somehow-- I'm not sure how we got the knitting needles, but we had knitting needles. Yes. I remember that distinctly. Both of my sisters and I knitted that pullover for him. And when it was finished, my middle sister, Manja-- Mary-- had a chance of giving it to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would have happened to you had they found you with these knitting needles and knitting a-- a sweater?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one day-- I was still working at the easier job than my sisters. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was woken up in the barracks by the leader of the camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she got me out of bed, and she asked me to open the cupboard where we kept our bread. You know, when you received a portion-- the daily bread, you had to make it last for breakfast, lunch, and dinner. So naturally we didn't eat it all. Some girls were so hungry that they wouldn't leave any. They just ate the bread up, you know, the minute they got it. But we kind of rationed ourselves. We portioned it, and we would leave some bread for the evening.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, they-- apparently somehow the German leader of the camp found out that we had different bread. We don't know how. And she came in shouting at me, where is the bread? Where is the bread? And I didn't know at-- at first what she was referring. Then I showed her the bread that we had from camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, no, the other bread. The other bread. Fortunately, my sisters took all of the bread. They ate it up the previous day, and there was no bread left of the kind that the Czechoslovakian man used to give us.

speaker: interviewee
text: She wanted to find out who was giving us bread and why we were accepting the bread. And she told me, in very loud voice, tell me the truth. Where is the bread? What did you do? And who gave it to you? And I said, I don't know. We don't have any bread. Any different bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she slapped me in the face very hard. She was very angry. And I just still didn't tell her where the bread came from. And there was no sign of any bread. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I suffered for a few days from the-- from hitting. But eventually it healed up. It was-- my face was swollen for a few days. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she--

speaker: interviewee
text: --life had to go on. And since then, we just made an end to accepting any bread from anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it offered, the bread?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall. I really am not quite sure of the continuation of it. I'm not really sure of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have only female guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had only female guards at-- later on, all the DP camps became [NON-ENGLISH]. Like labor camps became concentration camps. And I remember seeing a very high SS man who came to camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was the top SS man. At-- at that time it became really much stricter at Graeben. But we continued having a female DP camp leader.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a DP camp, a concentration camp leader. Sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what ways did it become more strict? Did your rations change? Did the guards change their attitudes?

speaker: interviewee
text: There-- there were more guards at work. There were-- the Germans became more [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] tyrannical. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a changed atmosphere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any specific actions of the guards that jump out at you now that you said, oh my gosh, they wouldn't have done that before, but look what they're doing now? Do you remember any of those?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they would send away girls who couldn't work. Girls who lost a lot of weight or ran a fever, they were sent-- sent to Auschwitz. We were told they were going to Auschwitz. And we knew at that time that Auschwitz was a bad place.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know exactly then. But two girls-- I would say women in their 30s, arrived in our camp with shaven heads. We still had our hair, and we kept ourself-- and we still had our own clothes from home. And these women, two women, were just completely different. Their looks and behavior was completely different than ours. They came in garments unlike ours, and shaven heads. And their behavior was different. We still had the ethics, and we still had our demeanor that we had at home. But these women seemed to be different. And we did know, as I mentioned, what Auschwitz was and that it was a bad place and what they were doing in Auschwitz. And one night, these-- these women were assigned to our room. And they were talking at night. And we heard them say, how naive they are. They don't know that their parents are dead, that their-- their-- that little kids are dead. That they could not understand that we didn't know anything about Auschwitz. But we, at that time, kind of found out the truth, the way they were talking. And later on, we were talking openly after that incident at night. They told us not as distinctly, but we should know that our parents are dead, that our younger sisters and brothers are dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever hear from your brothers or sisters or mother from the time you were in Graeben when they were still in Srodula?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to get postcards from my brother, my older brother. In fact, due to the fact that we had a Polish name, Zielinska, we would get mail from home which the Germans let through. They didn't know that it-- out of Sosnowiec Srodula, they didn't know that it was going to Jewish people. Some-- and we were called the three lucky Zielinskys in camp. [BACKGROUND NOISE] Bless you. And I-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in fact, I have a couple of postcards which my brother wrote to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hersh Mayer, the oldest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you have something that you'd like to read?

speaker: interviewee
text: Perhaps, if you'd like me to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: I better get my glasses.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are the postcards which-- my sister has a couple, and I have a couple of these cards. And it says, Graeben, Zielinska Fraulein E. Zielinska And this is another E. Zielinska, Graeben, near Schweidnitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I think you have--

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were addressed to Sarah, because they were-- the woman was given Sarah in addition to her own name, and the man was given Israel, so they would know. These were still OK without the Sarah and Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you have a portion that you've translated from Polish or-- or German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have--

speaker: interviewer
text: Are these-- were these written in Polish or German?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were written in Jewish-German, kind of, by my brother. And he says that he's very worried. He didn't get any mail from us. And so-- [SPEAKING GERMAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have the translation of any of-- or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I-- yes, I have the translation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Since letters were censored from-- from the ghetto, we had-- we had to kind of try and have some kind of words where the Germans wouldn't know what we were saying. To conceal what he was trying to tell us, 'headache' meant selection. And he says, 'I cannot comprehend what has happened to our family. I have such a headache--' which means there was a selection, '--but now I feel--' 'I have a headache, and the aspirin doctor had to visit me. But now I feel better. Dear God should help us that our whole family should be happily reunited in our home.'

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many of these letters did you receive over the course of time at Graeben?

speaker: interviewee
text: We received quite a few. And they kept on coming when nobody else was receiving letters. And that's why we were called the happy Zielinsky, three Zielinsky sisters. Somehow-- we suspect because of the name-- they let them go through, and then they gave them to us. They didn't realize that we were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you receive the letters?

speaker: interviewee
text: The letters kept coming through September, '43. And suddenly, we haven't-- they stopped coming. And at that stage, we felt that there was nobody left home. We later were told that they took our mother and the three younger children-- Abraham, Roizula, and Yentela, who were left. And my brothers-- we found that out after the war from someone who survived. And my two brothers stayed home. They-- they worked. And then they took them away. We were hoping that somehow they would survive, but they did not.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did they take them to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most likely Auschwitz or-- or Birkenau or the extermination camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you save these letters through the-- through Graeben?

speaker: interviewee
text: We saved these letters in our shoes. We-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we were then driven out of Graeben in-- in January, I think, '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, before you-- before you were driven out, you were able to save letters?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were able to save-- we had our-- still had our possessions in-- in Graeben. Excuse me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you kept the-- the post-- the letters in your shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: But we still had the little cupboards where we could keep things in Graeben.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you-- did you say you were made to leave Graeben?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then-- I think it was January, '45. We saw flames from a distance, because Graeben was not far from Gross-Rosen. And they were burn-- we-- we heard that they were burning the camp Gross-Rosen. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was burning the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans. The Germans. And we heard that the Russian army was approaching. I'm not sure how these rumors spread and where they came from. But then one day we were getting ready to go to work from the [GERMAN], from the yard, and they told us not to go back, that we are going someplace. And that's where they took us on a death march.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, let's take--

speaker: interviewee
text: They drove us out of Graeben.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Since they're driving you out of Graeben and we're going to start a new part of the story, let's take a moment and change tapes, and then we'll continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would appreciate that. Because I'm--
